student_name: João Guilherme da Costa e Silva
hub_subtitle: Business English técnico para reuniões com fornecedores internacionais, normas e entrevistas

lesson.menu_title: Navigating Conflict and Escalation -- Diplomatic Assertiveness in High-Stakes Negotiations
lesson.menu_desc: The fourth missed deadline: inversion for emphasis, the language of the contract and the escalation that keeps the supplier -- 40 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 4 -- Navigating Conflict and Escalation
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- João Guilherme | Aula 4 -- Navigating Conflict and Escalation

listenings.text: João, it is Katrin. I owe you an answer before Friday, and I would rather leave it here than in an email. First, the recovery plan. It is with our planning team tonight, and you will have it on Friday, with a date my own director has already agreed to defend. Second, force majeure. I took your argument to our legal department, and they agree with yours. The strike was announced six weeks in advance, so we are withdrawing the claim. Never before have we withdrawn a force majeure claim on a project of this size, and I wanted you to hear that from me and not from a lawyer. Third, and this is the difficult part. The indemnity in Clause 9 is still open, and my finance director will push back on it. I am not going to pretend otherwise. What I can tell you is that I would rather put a proposal forward and bite the bullet on the cost than have the two of us in arbitration next year. Talk on Friday.

Before you send that escalation email, three things about contracts, because engineers get these wrong more often than lawyers do. First. A penalty clause is not a weapon, it is a deterrent. The moment you invoke it, you have told the supplier that the relationship is over and that the lawyers are in charge. Hardly had we invoked the clause on the Line 2 package when the supplier stopped answering the phone, and every small favor we relied on disappeared. Use it late, and use it once. Second. Never confuse mediation with arbitration. In mediation, a neutral third party helps the two of you reach your own agreement, and either side can walk away. In arbitration, the third party decides for you, and the decision is binding. One is a conversation. The other is a judgment. Third. Write everything down. A promise on a call is not a binding agreement. The minutes you send afterwards, if nobody objects to them, very often are. And one last word about force majeure. It means an event that nobody could plan around. A strike announced six weeks in advance is not force majeure. It is a schedule risk, and the liability for it is contractual.

patterns.title: Same facts. Four different weights.
cards: You are telling the other side that this has no precedent, and that you are counting. "Never has this package been three weeks behind the baseline schedule."
You stack the evidence. It is the hardest sentence to answer, because there are two things to answer. "Not only did they miss the date, but they also sent an incomplete revision."
One problem on top of another
Two past events, almost simultaneous. It shows a pattern, not an accident. "Hardly had we begun the FAT when the connector failed."
The line you do not cross
The strongest refusal in professional English. Use it once, and mean it. "Under no circumstances will we waive the hold points."

patterns.text: Inversion is a register decision, not a grammar exercise. It says: I have read the contract, and this email can be forwarded to your director. Use it once or twice. Three times in one email and it sounds theatrical.

reading.rtitle: Escalation Note -- Signaling Package, Line 5 Extension (internal, Legal & Contracts)
paras: Never has a supplier on this package missed three consecutive deadlines. The revised baseline schedule was due on 10 June; it has now been outstanding for three weeks. Not only did the supplier fail to submit the schedule, but they also declined to confirm a new date in writing. Under Clause 14, the delay is a failure to meet a contractual obligation, and the penalty clause becomes applicable after thirty days.

The supplier states that the delay was caused by a strike at the port of Hamburg, and has therefore claimed force majeure. Legal's view is that the strike was announced six weeks in advance and could reasonably have been planned around. On that basis, force majeure is unlikely to apply, and liability for the delay remains with the supplier. Hardly had that argument been put forward when the supplier also asked for the indemnity in Clause 9 to be reviewed.

Motiva does not wish to pursue a formal complaint at this stage. The recommendation is to put forward a recovery plan, to make it clear that we are prepared to invoke the penalty clause, and to propose mediation before either party considers arbitration. Escalation is a double-edged sword: it protects the schedule, and it can cost us a supplier we will need again on Line 6. If the fourth deadline is missed, however, we bite the bullet. The minutes of the next call become a binding agreement, and the dispute resolution procedure begins.
reading.source: Motiva -- Legal & Contracts, internal note (fictional, written for João's package)

reading.text: Notice the collocations: invoke a penalty clause, claim force majeure, put forward a recovery plan, pursue a formal complaint, address contractual obligations, reach a resolution.

gist.prompt: What is the main idea of the note?
choices: The force majeure claim has been accepted, so the delay is excused and the penalty clause no longer applies.
Pressure with the door left open: a recovery plan and mediation now, the penalty clause and arbitration only if the fourth deadline is missed.
Motiva has decided to pursue a formal complaint and to begin arbitration immediately.
A strike at the port of Hamburg has delayed the signaling package by three weeks.

items: The penalty clause applies from the first day of delay.
f
"...the penalty clause becomes applicable after thirty days."
Legal believes the force majeure claim is unlikely to succeed.
t
The strike was announced six weeks in advance and could reasonably have been planned around.
Motiva wants to start arbitration immediately.
f
The recommendation is to propose mediation before either party considers arbitration.
Liability for the delay currently sits with the supplier.
t
"...liability for the delay remains with the supplier."
The note presents escalation as a purely positive step.
f
"Escalation is a double-edged sword: it protects the schedule, and it can cost us a supplier we will need again on Line 6."
The supplier asked for the indemnity in Clause 9 to be reviewed.
t
"Hardly had that argument been put forward when the supplier also asked for the indemnity in Clause 9 to be reviewed."

items.situation: "Hello João. I assume this is about the schedule again." Open the call. It is the third missed milestone.
tips: It would be remiss of us not to flag that this is the third missed milestone.
Never has this package been three weeks behind the baseline schedule.

items.situation: "The strike at the port was force majeure." The strike was announced six weeks in advance. Answer.
tips: One could argue that a strike announced six weeks in advance does not meet the definition of force majeure in Clause 14.
On that basis, liability for the delay remains with you.

items.situation: "So, are you going to invoke the penalty clause?" Be firm. Keep the door open.
tips: Under no circumstances do I want to start there.
But if the fourth deadline is missed, I'm afraid we're left with no alternative but to invoke it.

items.situation: "We sent you something last week." They sent the wrong revision AND they missed the date. Say both, with weight.
tips: Not only did they miss the date, but they also sent an incomplete revision.
No new date has been confirmed in writing either.

items.situation: "What exactly do you want, then?" Propose the way out before anyone mentions lawyers.
tips: Let me put forward a recovery plan.
We would much rather reach a resolution through mediation than pursue a formal complaint.

items.situation: "Fine. We are agreed." Close the call so that what you agreed actually counts.
tips: Can we treat the minutes of this call as a binding agreement?
I'll send them today, and I'll follow up on Friday.

bank.label: Opening the escalation
items: It would be remiss of us not to flag that this is the third missed milestone.
Never has this package been three weeks behind the baseline schedule.
I'd like to put the contractual position on the table before we discuss new dates.

bank.label: Stating the breach
items: Not only did the schedule slip, but no new date has been confirmed in writing.
Under Clause 14, this is a failure to meet a contractual obligation.
Three consecutive missed milestones may amount to a breach of contract.

bank.label: Pressure, with the door open
items: One could argue that a strike announced six weeks in advance is not force majeure.
Under no circumstances do I want to start there.
I'm afraid we're left with no alternative but to invoke the penalty clause.

bank.label: Proposing the way out
items: Let me put forward a recovery plan.
We would much rather reach a resolution through mediation than pursue a formal complaint.
Can we treat the minutes of this call as a binding agreement?
